Margin edged up a point, from twenty-one to twenty-two.
Sign-ups surged after the launch.
Revenue is up eleven per cent year on year.
Growth plateaued in the fourth quarter.
So, give me the quarter. How did the top line do?
The top line was ninety million, up eleven per cent year on year -- though on a like-for-like basis, stripping out the acquisition, it is closer to five.
Good, you gave me both. And the margin?
Gross margin edged up a point, from twenty-one to twenty-two per cent. Small, steady, driven by mix -- not a surge, and I would not call it one.
Your profit line looks wonderful, though. Too wonderful.
You are right to push. That beat is a one-off -- a tax gain that will not repeat. Underlying profit was broadly flat; higher costs were largely offset by higher volumes.
That is the honest read. So where is the story going?
We outperformed the sector on revenue, but cash collection is lagging the plan by two weeks. The momentum is real, and the one thing I am watching is that lag going into the fourth quarter.
The top line grew nine per cent year on year.
On a like-for-like basis, growth is closer to five per cent.
Gross margin edged up from twenty-one to twenty-two per cent.
The beat is a one-off; underlying profit was broadly flat.
We outperformed the sector, but cash collection is lagging the plan.

I have covered listed companies for fifteen years, and I will tell you the mistake I see finance chiefs make again and again. They read me a number. Revenue was ninety million. And they stop, as if the number were the point. But a single number is a photograph, and I do not invest in photographs. I invest in the film. So the question I am really asking, every time, is not what the number is, it is which way it is moving and how fast. Is it up year on year, or only up because last year was a disaster? Is the growth like for like, or did you just buy it? Did the margin edge up a point, or surge, or quietly plateau while nobody was looking? Tell me the direction, the speed, and the comparison, and I can price the business. Tell me only the number, and all you have given me is a still frame of something that is already moving. The best finance people I deal with never present a figure naked. They dress it in a trend and a comparison, because they know that on its own a number means almost nothing at all.

The hardest discipline in reporting performance is telling the truth about a good quarter. Anyone can spin a bad one. The temptation with a good one is to let the headline do the talking and hope nobody strips it back. So here is the rule I hold myself to. Before I present a single figure to the board, I separate the underlying performance from the one-offs. If revenue jumped, I ask myself honestly: is that real momentum, or did a delayed deal simply land in this quarter instead of the last one? If the margin looks wonderful, I check whether a cost was genuinely lower or merely offset by something that will reverse next period. And then I present both numbers, the headline and the underlying, side by side, because the board that trusts you in the good quarter is the board that believes you in the bad one. Precision is not pessimism. Describing performance accurately, with the right comparison and the honest caveat, is the single fastest way a finance chief earns the right to be believed.
